sentences.text: That's correct.
In the sense that there was a group of Jews, not very large, but there were Jews who were working and being paid in a place that was under German occupation--
Romanian.
And there were-- Ro--  Romanian occupation, but there were Germans around.
I will--   I will explain you.
And you were paying your rent in not very good accommodation.
But, I mean, it wasn't a barrack.
It wasn't a ghetto.
It wasn't a prison.
It was a ghetto.
Uh, the survivor-- we, originally, were about 100.
After the first winter, we shrunk the numbers to about 70.
They died.
And you ask how we paid.
It was not so-- paying.
We worked, you see.
If you worked, for instance, they liked to be Sunday off for their holidays.
We worked.
I worked in the stables.
They're only too glad when, in the night, I had remained in the stable with the-- with the horses or with the cows there.
You see.
And they did not work.
So I replaced them.
They were only too glad to give me, uh, some potatoes or some grain.
Yes?
I went to the mill, for instance.
And there, I helped to-- to work there.
So they made it-- flour for me.
Who ran the ghetto?
Uh, it was not run, you see.
My late father was the spokesman and, um, a Mr.
Hager, they were, let us call, the more older, but not decrepit old, you see.
And because he could speak the lingo, my father could speak with the-- all the languages which they spoke.
So he went in friendship with the mayor, as I told you.
And they liked us because we had, as I told, they were tradesman which could make the clothes, so usually, before, they had to go to [NON-ENGLISH] to make a coat.
So now they had it in the place.
And, uh, from our coat, they could make a coat for themself.
We had the tailors to make it.
Were the--  did the women work, as well?
All.
I told to you, they had, for the German army, to make gloves and sweaters.
And so our women worked because--
Yes.
We--  we were under, uh, punishment of death if you come over--  some of our--
Pardon?
They were Romanian soldiers-- Romanian-- in the local militia, Ukrainian militia.
Yes.
Uh, not with permission.
I went a few times, you see.
I was dressed as a Ukrainian.
And I risked because when my parents had typhoid and my sister, we needed to eat.
So I took flour, a bag of flour, in the bag, went in the ghetto Shargorod, exchange it there for soap, which the Jews produced there, in the ghetto.
You see, bartered it.
And came back, and I had, again, work to barter in [?
Losiva. ?] Who was guarding the Shargorod ghetto?
A-- again, militia.
And they had the Jewish militia there, Polizei.
Oh, about 1500.
There were quite a lot.
They were the-- mostly the deportees from [?Suceava?] plus other Jews.
And local Jews were also there, Ukrainian Jews.
Yes.
I went in the forest to work with a local Ukrainian, you see.
I worked.
And this time, I went to work for the school.
I was good-- in good relations with the headmaster.
[NON-ENGLISH], he was called.
[NON-ENGLISH], I give his complete name.
And they had to prepare, for the winter, some wood, timber.
So I went in the forest with one, the servant of the school, to cut from the young forest, in the hopes that I will meet some of the partisans there, which were in the region, acting, you see.
But I did not meet them at that time, and-- but I came on my way back, you see.
Uh, I met this carriage with two gendarmes, a Ukrainian driver, and my late father, my father.
But if you had permission to go and do that work, why were they looking--
I didn't have permission.
I didn't have permission.
But my-- they asked for me.
So my father had to tell them-- or-- or not-- my father had to go, you see.
So they went after me in the forest.
But in the [NON-ENGLISH], I was on the carriage coming back with [? days. ?]
Yes.
Yes.
And they put me in detention there, at the local jail in [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a class of the school.
Yes, yes.
To Shargorod with other young, uh, y-- young man when they-- all labor able men were taken away for forced labor.
First to Shargorod and then over Mogilev, [NON-ENGLISH] to Tulchyn.
Yes.
Because-- is the Russians liberated them, the territory.
Still in [?
Losiva ?] you see.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They survived by-- with the Ukrainian population.
They helped them.
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was 21st-- beginning of-- uh, end of March.
But they still stood on there.
March 1943.
Yes.
Yes.
They sent me with a-- a group which they formed for replenishment of the Semitic unit.

I was sent first to Proskurov, that night to Proskurov, and-- [? how ?] [? would ?] you say-- all mostly Ukrainian which were called up, former Soviet citizens, and soldiers which came back from hospital, wounded soldiers which were sent back from hospital to the front.
I came to Proskurov, and there, we were sent with-- to a unit.
The front was advancing near Brody.
That is in old Poland, already.
There it happened that we came about 7 o'clock in, uh, position.
I really did not know who is my commanding officer I had, who is my officers, company commander.
And we came to the unit, and they told us stay here-- these were trenches-- to stay in position here.
We were all armed.
The most had passed a training of a month or two in Kiev.
I was only one volunteered to the front, so I did not know the people around me.
I could speak with them.
They were Ukrainian or they are Russian.
And, uh, it was about 11 o'clock in the night.
We heard firing.
The Germans attacked.
We counterattacked.
It was forest there, young forest.
And I got lost.
I didn't know, really, where the front is, where my commanding officer is, where my colleagues are, my mates are.
And I got lost.
I decided to wait till the morning for-- shooting from every side, artillery and machine gun fire.
So I decided to stay there.
About an hour or two later, another soldier-- Soviet, in Soviet uniform-- appeared-- Red Army uniform-- appeared.
So I joined him.
We opened the [INAUDIBLE] it together.
And towards the morning, a sergeant, Red-- of the Red Army-- appeared.
And he was-- this is called conscripting, assembling, uh, soldiers for return-- dissent, stragglers in the forest for return, dissent.
So I was-- was glad to have a commander.
So I went after him.
He put us-- his tank.
And we broke through to Brody, that was quite a town in former Poland, Galicia.
There, we didn't stop.
We were three tanks which broke through the lines.
And we went on, straight on-- it's about 30 miles-- to, uh, towards Lviv.
That is the capital of Galicia, a large town.
Now we enter the town, the Germans were in the town.
We stopped in front-- I remember well-- and stopped in front of the prison, local prison.
There were, uh, Ukrainian guards there.
German, I didn't see.
Perhaps there were.
We stormed the prison.
It was an, uh, idea of mine to storm the prison because we were only three tanks.
And we were only three tanks, were about 50 soldiers infan-- infantry.
We opened the gates, they finished off the guards and took their arms.
And we kept the town till about-- in the evening.
More tanks arrived.
And we started cleaning the town.
But they found we are so good, so they decided to go ahead.
It was quick-moving front.
We went towards the Sian, the river Sian.
In the mean time, I got the rank of sergeant, my first advancement in the army.
And, uh, we went into Sian, [INAUDIBLE] was bitter fighting.
We crossed the Sian, the river Sian.
They didn't expect us.
It was a hill up in the town, toward the marketplace.
And I was only a few hours in the town.
We were sitting and waiting for replenishment, other soldiers.
But I was called with the other two, three soldiers and sent to-- the commandant wants to speak to us.
And he said, you are good soldiers.
Look, you have here an order to go to Rovno.
That is in Volhynia.
He said, in Russia, you don't go by Rolls-Royce, and so you-- you thought you find a carriage, a truck which goes the direction you're taking.
And you-- so I went to Rovno.
In Rovno, I announced myself to Commandant [INAUDIBLE] that I was nearly killed because the Stukas appeared and bombed us.
I didn't know where to run.
So I was laying near some tanks with one of the-- my friends from the old-- old-- old unit.
We, again, tried to eat in the house which was there.
What happened, happened.
And when I turned my head towards him-- we were speaking-- his head was gone.
You can imagine sitting in-- in Rovno or where.
When they came back, they gave me an order to go again for [NON-ENGLISH] in Volodymyr-Volynskyi.
From Volodymyr-Volynskyi, they had a-- a unit which was formed in the forest.
That was a special unit, a special commando unit from special operation behind the front and on the front line.
So I reached my 81st military special [INAUDIBLE] unit.
With this I stood, I was, up to the end of the war.
Up to-- from my killing, if I could call it so, when I was brought from the peasant.
It was in November 1942 up to, uh, '43, 1943 in March, when I came back to Chernovitz, the liberation of Chernovitz.
Oh, '44, sorry, '44, it was. '44 because we were operating the whole summer with them. '44, it was.
Over a year.
One and a half year.
Not.
Nobody knew except my commanding officer.
Uh, I told him because he interrogated me, you see.
Uh, he interrogated me, and he-- so that I am all right, you see, I'm good for-- I will help them.
Sorry.
And the [INAUDIBLE] knew.
Uh, he-- I told him.
I didn't have to hide.
There was three days, and he asked me, how are you?
And I told him, I'm-- I am a deportee.
I'm a Jew and a deportee.
They deported us.
And, uh, so he treated me-- he treated me well, you see.
He did all what he could to help me.
Yes.
Ukrainian.
Ukrainian.
The [INAUDIBLE] was, really, originally from Bessarabia.
But he could speak a bit Romanian, a few words.
But the commanding officer was a higher party.
He was a head of a kolkhoz somewhere in Ukraine in Vinnitsa region.
And he organized this group under the higher group of [NON-ENGLISH].
It's become after again [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
Uh, you see, he-- I could help them.
You see, I know languages.
They could use me spying on the Germans.
I could enter in any town as a Ukrainian boy, speaking Ukrainian.
And they wouldn't recognize.
I was dressed in the [NON-ENGLISH].
They could not recognize that I'm a Jew.
Practically, there were no Jews around.
And I helped them.
Not.
[NON-ENGLISH] was my name, Ukrainian.
In the partisans, they told me to change.
They gave me the name.
The paramedic suggested it, you see, the name.
Yes.
They told me not to say it.
You see, I worked first with the-- it's a-- anesthetist, if I could call it so.
Helped him in the operation, which he had surgery, which he had, primitive.
Uh, my anesthetic was in a wooden hammer, a mallet.
Had-- bang over the head.
And that, I succeeded in knocking out.
Or if it was a VIP, then, a bit of vodka helped us.
And what sort of terrain where you operating in, mainly, at this time?
In Vinnitsa Oblast, Galicia--
German, German occupied.
German country.
Forest.
That was flat country in Ukraine.
But as-- as we came nearer to the Carpathians, started hilly country in the mountain.
Not.
They-- in some places, we met other partisan group which helped us.
Some local populations, villages, helped us.
Others, called the German, you see.
We had to kill their militia, you see.
And some were pro and some were against, you see.
But as we came near the Carpathians, the German, there were mostly SS units operating there.
As I heard afterwards, uh, they tried-- there, they didn't have, really, military units which were on the front.
So-- but they had SS schools, infantry school, [INAUDIBLE] school, but SS, Waffen-SS.
And they used them to hunt us.
And they got on the idea to pay the local who told.
For every partisan they bring, they give them salt or sugar, which were good for barter.
So they committed treason.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
I was, I remember, uh, [NON-ENGLISH] I remember it was the name.
He was a secretary of a-- a [NON-ENGLISH].
And he--
District.
He was a Russian citizen, Soviet citizen, who-- party member who was left with money to organize the underground.
He used this underground-- uh, this money for the private purposes.
Built himself a nice house and so on.
But he-- I don't think he knew his son-in-law was a member of the underground.
And he said he is doing the things with the Romanians and the-- and the Germans.
So it was a partisan court.
He was condemned and executed.
This I saw.
And others, in many villages-- usually, we did not use, only if it was quickly to do.
They were hanged as a punishment, 'traitor' here, written.
And he was hanged.
They were hanged.
We had to meet chiefs of militia, oh, militia [INAUDIBLE], which were on the German side.
[COUGHS] They were given house often by the local population, which hated them, you see.
The Germans started taking young people to work in Germany.
And we got the order-- I don't know who from-- to cut the lines of the railway lines to stop the trains.
Some of them, of the young people which were on the train, joined us and were in the partisan [? ranks, ?] boys and girls.
They were from different parts-- from Kiev, from [NON-ENGLISH], from [NON-ENGLISH], and the German occupation.
And they joined us.
Our unit were about 400.
Sometimes we-- we were-- when we came back, we were 27, when we came back from the Carpathians.
We were-- we were decimated there.
Fighting was bitter there.
Not ranks, really.
Everybody had a job, you see.
My job, I was [NON-ENGLISH], This is reconnaissance, a reconnaissance.
First I was aiding the [INAUDIBLE] until I could run, and to learn mine, to put mine in this.
You had to learn.
This is not in one hour.
Yes.
I, myself, was interested to-- to learn this, you see, and to-- to fight.
Not.
I did not meet.
I-- we met, the whole group met-- it was near Buchach, if I remember well-- a group of Hungarian, uh, [NON-ENGLISH] soldiers, they were called.
They were labor, forced labor, if I could call it so.
But the uniforms were Hungarian uniform.
There were some Jews.
They didn't understand Yiddish.
I could not speak to them, also.
But my commanding officer take-- he told me, perhaps you will find volunteers.
Speak to them.
They were thinking that I know every language, um, speaking.
With the [NON-ENGLISH], with the Italians we had, also, there on the front-- and I could speak to them.
So they were thinking I speak-- speak to them.
And-- but nobody wanted, not one wanted because they were Jews.
They did not want to go with us.
German.
Some of them knew German.
And some were from Slovakia.
You could speak Ukrainian, and it was a similar language you could speak to them.
And-- but they-- not one joined.
Not my unit.
Not my unit.
Not my unit.
There were in other units, I heard afterwards.
But I did not meet, officially, in the unit.
No, but, possibly, they could have been in hiding this, in terms of their identity, in the same way as you.
Or do you think you would have recognized them.
Uh, not because after a time, being all the time together, operating together with the group, uh, you feel with a Jew or not.
You understand?
Their way of thinking is differently-- I don't know.
You feel it.
[SIGHS] Ah, it hurt when you came in a place-- I was in a place.
Afterwards, I recognize some people from my town.
I-- it was in the fields.
They were killed, also.
That was not long time ago, when I was in the partisans.
They were taking over the [? book ?] from some other places where they were deported.
And I heard afterwards, I-- I-- aunt, a cousin, second-grade cousin and her husband, which I knew very well, they were also, between the victims we found.
And I recognize they are Jews.
I recognize some of the faces.
They were shot by the Germans.
Not.
They were not interested.
You see, they were interested in liberate the Ukraine, and they got-- they had a radio station, which I couldn't go to hear.
Yes?
We had the connection with [NON-ENGLISH], the great Earth.
It was a free country.
And they asked-- with the radio, they asked to drop ammunition or this, about.
Dropped.
Or from the Germans.
Or dropped by plane.
Or we had to-- to take them.
It was a time in Berdychiv, was a Slovak unit.
The commanding officer was, uh, a connection with us.
And he supplied us with bandages, some [INAUDIBLE], oh, uh, some ammunition.
You see, he hated the Germans.
And he also sometimes told-- there is German [INAUDIBLE] with ammunition and food.
We go in this direction.
So we had our connection with him and-- and operated [INAUDIBLE].
They didn't speak, and I didn't ask.
You see, I could not.
They would have known.
What is your interest in Jews?
You understand?
But I mean, generally, against Ukrainians or-- or Russians if they--
They-- those who were with us hated the Germans, you see.
Some, uh, had their parents or relatives killed, or they were sent off to labor force and escaped, as I told you before.
They were not friends of the Germans, see.
Some were, uh, [NON-ENGLISH], this is young communists.
And they fought with-- some were party members, you see.
But all of their fighting them, for me, it was-- I saved my life.
Yes.
They saved my life.
And I fought the common enemy, if I could call it like this.
